data.title: Blackstone Q1 earnings: Focus on AUM inflows, private equity outlook
data.description:
Blackstone (NYSE:BX) will report its first-quarter earnings on April 17, and investors will keep an eye on fee-related earnings and AUM growth.  

The consensus EPS estimate is $1.07 (+9.2% Y/Y) and the consensus revenue estimate is $2.72B.

"Blackstone is set to report Q1 2025 earnings with mixed signals; a strong asset base but numerous downward EPS revisions suggest a tough quarter," Seeking Alpha analyst Julia Ostian said.

Despite recent upgrades and a solid dividend yield, Blackstone's high payout ratio and rich valuation pose risks amid cooling market conditions, Ostian said, adding, "Key metrics to watch include fee-related earnings, AUM inflows, and realized performance revenue, with management's outlook on private equity and real estate crucial."

The asset manager mainly focuses on 4 core areas of investment: real estate, private equity, credit & insurance, and hedge funds.

Earlier in April, the firm was upgraded by Citizens to Market Outperform. In March, UBS upgraded Blackstone (NYSE:BX) to Buy from Neutral as the stock's recent 27% slump provides an entry point for a "premier alts [alternatives] platform with structural and scale advantages at a reasonable valuation."

UBS also figures that headwinds to Blackstone's (BX) real estate business have diminished, allowing for potential fee-related performance revenue contributions as early as 2025.

Blackstone (BX) is also reportedly considering acquiring a small minority stake in the U.S. operations of TikTok.

"Blackstone's diversified private markets platform and leading position in the industry make it a compelling buy, especially given the current attractive valuation and 4.5% dividend yield," Seeking Alpha analyst Perseus Perspectives said.

"The firm’s fee-earning AUM provides a stable revenue floor, ensuring dependable earnings even during market downturns, with significant growth potential in the private wealth channel," Perseus Perspectives added.

Seeking Alpha analyst Jonathan Weber believes declining interest rates are boosting demand for alternative investments, benefiting Blackstone's inflows and overall performance.

Last quarter, the private equity giant delivered stronger-than-expected Q4 earnings, as assets under management climbed, bolstered by strong inflows and a 14% Y/Y increase in management and advisory fees.

Over the last 2 years, BX has beaten EPS estimates 75% of the time and has beaten revenue estimates 25% of the time.

Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 0 upward revisions and 17 downward. Revenue estimates have seen 0 upward revisions and 4 downward.

Shares in the company were down 24.6% since the start of the year.

data.title: Cloudflare rises after upgrade at Mizuho
data.description:
Shares of Cloudflare (NYSE:NET) rose about 3% on Wednesday after Mizuho upgraded the stock to Outperform from Neutral citing growing AI traction and the company being poised for acceleration. 

The firm, however, cut the price target to $135 from $140 on the shares of the cloud security solutions provider.

The analysts said their recent checks on Cloudflare were more favorable than they expected, and included strengthening pipelines, large-deal activity, and growing traction with Workers AI.

More broadly, the analysts believe Cloudflare has a highly scalable architecture and a culture of strong innovation, and they are bullish on the AI inferencing opportunity.

Despite higher macro uncertainty, the analysts said, they have a little more confidence in the multi-year acceleration story at Cloudflare, and with the shares having declined 38% since mid-February (versus the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) and Nasdaq down mid/high teens), they find the risk/reward attractive.

data.title: Spotify facing outage in major markets; company 'working on a fix'
data.description:
• Online music platform Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) is experiencing an increase in outages on Wednesday, according to Downdetector.
  • The website showed that over 48,000 users reported outages in the U.S. by 9:38 am ET.
  • About 57% of the users who reported an outage were facing trouble using the app.
  • At 9:54 am ET, Spotify posted on X, "Hi everyone! We're aware of issues affecting the Spotify app and website loading. The right team is on it and working on a fix."
  • The platform is also seeing outages in major markets like the UK, Canada, and Brazil.

data.title: Sunnova struggling to win creditor lifeline to avert bankruptcy - Bloomberg
data.description:
Sunnova Energy (NYSE:NOVA) -7.6% to an all-time intraday low $0.20 in early trading Wednesday, a day after Bloomberg reported the seller of rooftop solar panels is having difficulty convincing creditors to provide it with fresh cash so it can avoid bankruptcy, and has all but ceased to originate new business.

Sunnova (NYSE:NOVA) has been in talks with its creditors for the past month, but efforts to raise new funds have been unsuccessful, and lenders are concerned that the substantial level of financing the company is seeking would do little to turn around the business, given in part its reliance on federal tax incentives that may be at risk, according to the report.

Still, no final decision on a course of action has been made, and the company's fate remains fluid, the report said.

Sunnova (NOVA) issued a going concern warning last month, its CEO and CFO have stepped down, it has brought in two new board members with restructuring experience, and it has missed an interest payment on 11.75% senior notes due 2028, and a 30-day grace period to make good on the amount due is coming to an end.

data.title: ASML falls after bookings miss estimates, CEO warns of tariff uncertainty
data.description:
Shares of ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) fell 5.7% on Wednesday after the company warned that tariffs were increasing uncertainty for the long term.

Tariffs and outlook

“There's this new uncertainty around tariffs. And like many experts, many businesses are explaining this is, of course, something that we don't know how to quantify yet,” said CEO Christophe Fouquet in a video uploaded on ASML's website. “But this is adding definitely uncertainty on the long term.” 

ASML's CFO Roger Dassen broke down four categories by which tariffs could impact the company: shipments to the U.S.; tariffs on imported parts and tools; material imports required for its manufacturing in the U.S.; and tariffs from other countries on U.S. exports.

“We're very actively working with the entire ecosystem to try and minimize the overall impact on the whole ecosystem as a result of that,” Dassen explained.

Dassen added it was too early to discuss what extent of impact tariffs would have on global GDP and total market demand, “and therefore it's quite impossible actually to put a number on that.”

Outlook

ASML kept its 2025 total net sales outlook unchanged, and expects it to between €30B and €35B, with the mid-point at €32.5B.

ASML expects second quarter total net sales to be between €7.2B and €7.7B, with the mid-point at €7.45B.

Gross margins are expected to be between 50% and 53%, with Evercore attributing roughly 100 basis points worth of impact to tariffs. The investment firm reiterated its Outperform rating and €965 price target on the stock.

Despite the concern about tariffs, ASML sees strength in AI, Fouquet said. “In fact some of the demand for this year, of course, but also for next year has solidified,” he noted. “So that's very encouraging.” 

Key metrics

The Dutch company — which makes extreme ultraviolet lithography, or EUV, machines — saw first quarter net sales grow about 46.3% year-over-year to €7.74B, which missed estimates. Compared to the fourth quarter of 2024, net sales fell 16.4%.

The company reported bookings of €3.94B, below the average estimate of €4.82B by analysts, according to Bloomberg News. Net bookings for EUV machines were €1.2B.

ASML said in January that it would stop reporting order bookings after 2025.

China sales

China accounted for 27% of the total net system sales of about €5.742B, behind the South Korea with 40% share in the first quarter of 2025. U.S. and Taiwan had 16% share, each in the first quarter of 2025.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, China accounted for 27% of the total net system sales. At the time, Fouquet said he believed China would go back to a more normal ratio in their business.

The U.S. has placed several export restrictions on companies, including ASML, to curb China's access to advanced semiconductor equipment, which, among other things, is used in making AI products.

Stock buyback

ASML bought around 4M shares for about €2.7B in the first quarter.

Update: The story was updated with Evercore analyst commentary. 

More on ASML

  • ASML Holding: A Strong Buy Into Earnings
  • ASML: I'm Not Buying The Dip -Stock Headed For The Low $400s
  • ASML On The Verge Of Losing EUV Monopoly
  • European markets dip as investors remain cautious with tariff turbulence, ASML falls
  • ASML GAAP EPS of €6.00 beats by €0.20, revenue of €7.74B misses by €40M; initiates Q2 outlook

data.title: Urban-Gro wins three new Fogo de Chao restaurant contracts
data.description:
• urban-gro (NASDAQ:UGRO) has announced that the firm has secured three new contracts with the internationally renowned restaurant from Brazil, Fogo de Chão.
  • The Co. has been awarded a comprehensive construction contract for a new restaurant in Murray, Utah, where they will oversee the entire project lifecycle from preconstruction to post-construction phases.
  • Additionally, the Co. has also been awarded two Construction Management contracts, taking a leadership role in overseeing the construction process while acting as the owner's representative.
  • In this capacity, the Co. will define project objectives, deliverables, and timelines to ensure smooth execution for new restaurant locations in Las Vegas, Nevada and Rancho Cucamonga, California.

data.title: India is said to court defense customers with global financing push
data.description:
As geopolitical tensions drive global demand for military hardware, India is expanding efforts to position itself as a competitive arms exporter by offering concessional loans and targeting markets traditionally aligned with Russia, Reuters reported Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

In a bid to elevate its presence in the global defense industry, the Indian government is expanding the role of its state-owned Export-Import Bank (EXIM) to provide long-term, low-interest financing to international buyers.

Those potential customers include those with limited access to conventional credit because of elevated political or financial risk. This strategy is part of a broader initiative to double India’s defense exports to $6 billion by 2029, Reuters reported.

Targeting Russia’s customers

India’s strategy zeroes in on countries that historically depended on Russia for military supplies. With Moscow increasingly focused on its own war demands and Western suppliers stretched thin, New Delhi sees an opportunity to capture market share.

Defense attachés are being dispatched to embassies in key regions in Africa, South America and Southeast Asia to directly promote Indian-made weapons systems and evaluate host countries’ procurement needs, Reuters reported.

A notable example is Armenia, where India now accounts for 43% of recent arms imports, up from negligible levels just a few years ago. Similar efforts are underway in Brazil, where India is marketing its Akash missile system and exploring opportunities to build naval vessels.

Financing as a differentiator

Unlike many private banks that avoid high-risk clients, EXIM’s expanding portfolio, valued at $18.3 billion in fiscal years 2023–24, offers a crucial edge.

Indian arms manufacturers have actively lobbied for broader access to EXIM-backed financing to compete with the export-credit-backed deals offered by defense exporters in France, China and Turkey.

While most EXIM activity is conducted through its commercial division, the Indian government acts as a financial backstop, minimizing the risk to private lenders. This structure gives India a way to support large-scale deals that might otherwise stall due to credit constraints in target markets.

Cost-effective supplier

India is also leveraging its cost advantages. According to sources familiar with recent deals cited by Reuters, Indian 155mm artillery shells can be produced for $300 to $400 apiece, compared with more than $3,000 for equivalent European products. Similarly, Indian howitzers are priced at roughly half the cost of Western counterparts.

Defense producers such as Munitions India, Adani Defence and SMPP are scaling up production to meet growing demand, with SMPP already preparing a new facility dedicated to large-caliber ammunition, Reuters reported.

Implications for investors

India’s expanding defense footprint may offer long-term growth for investors focused on the aerospace and defense sector. Key domestic players such as Bharat Electronics, Adani Defence and SMPP – whose shares aren’t listed in U.S. markets – are poised to gain from any rising export volumes, government-backed financing and expanded international marketing efforts.

Of course, there are risks. Gaining entry to new markets with high-end systems requires sustained investment in R&D, ongoing diplomacy and a proven record of fulfilling orders. Meanwhile, geopolitics and competition from established defense exporters could influence deal flow.

It's important for investors to keep an eye on India's ambitions to become a major arms exporter. The shifts in global defense supply chains — especially as U.S. and European suppliers prioritize domestic rearmament — could give Indian companies an opening that affects more established rivals.

data.title: U.S. recession would deepen budget deficit despite lower interest payments, Apollo warns
data.description:
As concerns grow over a potential U.S. recession, new analysis by Apollo reveals that even with falling interest rates, the federal budget deficit could worsen significantly.

If the U.S. enters a recession, long-term interest rates are likely to go down, and it would be cheaper for the U.S. government to refinance existing government debt Torsten Sløk, Apollo’s chief economist stated. However, Sløk cautioned that any potential savings would be eclipsed by broader economic consequences. 

According to Apollo, a two percentage point drop in interest rates could save the federal government approximately $500B annually in interest payments. But the picture isn't that simple. Historically, recessions deepen the U.S. budget deficit by about 4% of GDP due to falling tax revenues and increased government spending on safety net programs like unemployment benefits. 

In 2025 dollars, the investment institution said this would equate to a staggering $1.3T hit to government finances which is more than double the savings from lower interest rates. 

“The bottom line is that it is not possible to improve the budget deficit by creating a recession because during a recession government finances would deteriorate by double the amount saved in interest payments,” Sløk concluded. 

Economists warn that any short-term gains in borrowing costs could be overshadowed by long-term fiscal strain.

Market Tracking ETFs: (NYSEARCA:DIA), (NYSEARCA:DDM), (NYSEARCA:DOG), (NYSEARCA:DXD), (NYSEARCA:SDOW), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), (NYSEARCA:SPXU), (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).
